items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Central African Republic: Overview of incidents affecting humanitarian workers (January - June 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Central African Republic
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

CONTEXT

Since January 2026, 53 incidents have affected humanitarian workers in the Central African Republic. In June, 17 incidents were reported, compared with six in May, representing an 89 per cent increase, linked to the conflict dynamics in the south-east and north-east.

14 incidents of criminal nature were reported, including eight armed robberies, three intrusions, two thefts, and one humanitarian aid looting. The remaining incidents involved one illegal taxation, interference with humanitarian activities, and one case of abduction of a humanitarian staff. At least one humanitarian worker was injured.

Birao was the most affected sub-prefecture, with five incidents, followed by Zémio with four incidents. The sub-prefectures of Obo and Bossangoa recorded three incidents each, while two incidents were reported in each of the sub-prefectures of Nana-Bakassa, Bangui-Rapide, Bangui-Fleuve and Ouadda. International NGOs were the most affected, with 10 incidents, followed by national NGOs with six incidents. One United Nations agency was also affected.

Since January 2026, Haut-Mbomou, Bangui, and Vakaga have remained the most affected prefectures, with 14 incidents recorded in Haut-Mbomou and 11 incidents in each of the remaining prefectures.

Country: Central African Republic
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: CAR: République centrafricaine : Aperçu des incidents ayant affecté les travailleurs humanitaires (janvier-juin 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Central African Republic
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

CONTEXTE

Depuis janvier 2026, 53 incidents ont affecté les travailleurs humanitaires en République centrafricaine. En juin, 17 incidents ont été signalés, contre neuf en mai, soit une hausse de 89%, particulièrement liée aux dynamiques du conflit dans le sud-est et dans le nord-est.

14 incidents de nature criminelle ont été rapportés, dont huit braquages à main armée, trois intrusions, deux vols et un pillage de l’aide humanitaire. Les autres incidents comprennent une taxation, une interférence et un cas d’enlèvement d’un acteur humanitaire. Au moins un travailleur humanitaire a été blessé.

La sous-préfecture de Birao a été la plus affectée, avec cinq incidents, suivie de Zémio avec quatre incidents. Les sous-préfectures de Obo et Bossangoa ont enregistré trois incidents chacune, tandis que deux incidents ont été rapportés dans chacune des sous-préfecture Nana-Bakassa, Bangui-Rapide, Bangui-Fleuve et Ouadda. Les ONG internationales sont les plus affectées, avec 10 incidents, suivies des ONG nationales avec six incidents. Une agence des Nations Unies a également été affectée.

Depuis janvier 2026, le Haut-Mbomou, Bangui, et la Vakaga restent les préfectures les plus affectées, avec respectivement 14, et 11 incidents dans chacune des deux dernières préfectures.

items.creator: ChildFund International
items.title: Venezuela Earthquake: Flash Update #2 (9th July 2026)
items.author: ChildFund International
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: ChildFund International

Please refer to the attached file.

Two weeks after the 24 June seismic doublet (magnitude 7.2 and 7.5, 39 seconds apart), Venezuela's emergency has shifted from acute search and rescue into sustained, multi-sector humanitarian operations, even as rescuers occasionally still find survivors and already-weakened buildings continue to collapse.

The official death toll stands at 3,535, with 16,740 people injured and nearly 18,000 displaced. Authorities now report more than 940 aftershocks. Buildings damaged are reported between 856 (government) and 2,501 (OCHA/ partner tracking), including 190 confirmed collapses.

More than 55,000 people remain unaccounted for on the national missing-persons platform, of which 123 are children. Authorities report 80 operational transitional camps supporting the displaced population.

Independent satellite-based analysis (ANOVA Policy Research, with Microsoft AI for Good) suggests population exposure in La Guaira's coastal corridor may be up to three times higher than official figures capture, due to outdated census frameworks and under-counted floating population. Of an estimated 403,784 residents across seven La Guaira parishes, roughly 73,500 lived in structures with observable damage, and 37,600 in structures with severe damage or collapse — the group most likely to need transitional or permanent housing.

The UNDRR assessment estimates approximately USD 37 billion in direct damage economy-wide, of which roughly USD 24 billion is concentrated in housing, businesses, schools, hospitals, and public facilities, alongside a separate preliminary UNDP estimate of USD 6.7 billion, equivalent to 6% of GDP.

The official death toll stands at 3,535, with 16,740 people injured and nearly 18,000 displaced. Authorities now report more than 940 aftershocks. Buildings damaged are reported between 856 (government) and 2,501 (OCHA/ partner tracking), including 190 confirmed collapses.
More than 55,000 people remain unaccounted for on the national missing-persons platform, of which 123 are children. Authorities report 80 operational transitional camps supporting the displaced population.
Independent satellite-based analysis (ANOVA Policy Research, with Microsoft AI for Good) suggests population exposure in La Guaira's coastal corridor may be up to three times higher than official figures capture, due to outdated census frameworks and under-counted floating population. Of an estimated 403,784 residents across seven La Guaira parishes, roughly 73,500 lived in structures with observable damage, and 37,600 in structures with severe damage or collapse — the group most likely to need transitional or permanent housing.

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: Tanzania: Evaluation of the Farm to Market Alliance (FtMA) Programme for Strengthening Inclusive Markets and Partnerships for Sustainable Food Systems (2022-2025)
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: United Republic of Tanzania
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

This report provides an independent assessment of the Farm to Market Alliance (FtMA) Phase II Programme (2022–2025). It presents key findings, lessons learned, and recommendations from the decentralized evaluation of FtMA was implemented across six regions.

The Tanzania FtMA Phase II programme is a public–private, multi-stakeholder platform managed by WFP, designed to enhance the resilience of smallholder farmers (SHFs) by improving access to productivity-enhancing services and structured offtake markets. Interventions were organized around four pillars: (i) Farmer Service Centre (FSC) network building; (ii) enhancing farmer productivity; (iii) market linkages; and (iv) farmer and FSC digitalization.

The evaluation serves two complementary purposes: accountability and learning. Regarding accountability, it assessed the extent of achieving intended results and whether resources were used efficiently and appropriately. From a learning perspective, the evaluation identified good practices, operational challenges, and strategic lessons.

Country: United Republic of Tanzania

items.creator: International Rescue Committee
items.title: Bangladesh: Heavy monsoons claim the lives of Rohingya refugees in Cox’s Bazar refugee camp | The IRC
items.author: International Rescue Committee
items.content:
Countries: Bangladesh, Myanmar
Source: International Rescue Committee
  • Major landslides have claimed the lives of at least 14 Rohingya refugees, including five children who were seeking shelter in a madrasa on Wednesday afternoon.
  • Nearly 1 million Rohingya refugees live in overcrowded camps built on steep, landslide-prone terrain, where every monsoon season brings the risk of deadly landslides and flooding.
  • The IRC is supporting affected families alongside camp authorities and urges donors to scale up funding in order to help prevent future disasters.

Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh, July 9, 2026 — At least 17 people have died after heavy rains have caused major landslides in Cox’s Bazar, including 14 Rohingya refugees. On Wednesday, a landslide struck a madrasa in Camp 5, killing at least five children with fears for others who may remain trapped. The International Rescue Committee (IRC) is supporting affected families as rescue operations continue.

Cox's Bazar hosts nearly 1 million Rohingya refugees who fled violence in Myanmar, living in some of the most densely populated conditions in the world on hilly, erosion-prone terrain. Each monsoon season, landslides and flooding destroy shelters and claim lives across the camps, and recent reductions in humanitarian funding have strained the services families rely on to prepare for and recover from such disasters.

"Every monsoon season, families in Cox's Bazar are fearful that the ground beneath them will give way. The children sheltering inside a madrasa during Wednesday's landslide were in a place that should have been among the safest in the camp," said Subarna Barma, IRC Head of Emergency and Resilience in Bangladesh. “Families who fled unimaginable violence in Myanmar are now burying their children, while rescuers dig for those still missing.

“The IRC is working alongside the Government of Bangladesh, camp authorities, local partners and refugee volunteers to reach families hit by the disaster. But response alone is not enough. With the monsoon already deadly and an El Niño season raising the risk of further extremes, we are calling on donors and the international community to urgently step up funding for the Rohingya response by investing not just in relief, but in the things that save lives before the next storm hits, including safer sites, climate-resilient infrastructure, early warning systems and protection for refugees and their host communities alike."

Rescue operations are ongoing, led by the Fire Service and Civil Defence Department, with support from humanitarian actors and camp authorities. The number of casualties is expected to rise as search efforts continue. The IRC is continuing to deliver essential services, including psychological first aid and protection monitoring, alongside raising awareness among communities of landslide risks. The landslide underscores the dangers facing children in the camps, where families live in bamboo and tarpaulin shelters on slopes that become unstable during the monsoon, and where safe communal spaces for children remain limited.

The IRC began responding to the Rohingya crisis in August 2017 and launched its response officially in March 2018. With over 400 staff in Bangladesh and operating across 33 camps across the division, our teams provide essential healthcare to the host community as well as Rohingya population in Cox’s Bazar, as well as reproductive and maternal healthcare, child protection, education, prevention and response to Gender-Based Violence, and Emergency Disaster Risk Reduction (EDRR).

items.creator: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.title: World: The State of Agricultural Commodity Markets 2026
items.author: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.content:
Country: World
Source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations

Please refer to the attached file.

Food and agricultural trade increased fivefold between 2000 and 2024, with low- and middle-income countries becoming more integrated into global markets. Yet these markets face growing exposure to shocks, including extreme weather, conflicts, pandemics, macroeconomic pressures and financial crises. The 2026 edition of The State of Agricultural Commodity Markets examines how such shocks affect trade, how disruptions are transmitted through trade linkages, and what this means for global food security. It highlights the need for trade policies that reflect the interdependence of agrifood systems, strengthen resilience and help mitigate the effects of shocks on food markets and food security.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: World
Source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations

items.creator: Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre
items.title: The human cost of disaster displacement in Bangladesh: A life-year approach to understanding impacts
items.author: Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre
items.content:
Country: Bangladesh
Source: Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre

Please refer to the attached file.

Using a new methodology, this report estimates the human cost of disaster displacement in Bangladesh and shows how this evidence can inform disaster risk reduction, recovery planning and investment decisions.

Bangladesh is among the countries most affected by disaster displacement worldwide. Between 2008 and 2025, disasters, primarily cyclones, floods and storm surges, triggered more than 21 million movements. For many people, internal displacement is a recurrent shock, disrupting housing, livelihoods, access to services and overall wellbeing and generating substantial human costs. In Bangladesh, disaster displacement is estimated to have resulted in around 89,000 life-years lost between 2008 and 2025, equivalent to about $1.7 billion in cumulative human costs or an average of $102 million a year.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Bangladesh
Source: Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre

Please refer to the attached file.

items.creator: Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre
items.title: World: A life-year approach for measuring the human cost of disaster displacement
items.author: Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre
items.content:
Country: World
Source: Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre

Please refer to the attached file.

This methodological note introduces a new methodology for estimating the human costs of internal displacement using a life-year approach. Developed for researchers and practitioners, it provides a framework for quantifying the impacts of displacement to inform policies and investment decisions.

Internal displacement impacts housing, livelihoods, health, and overall wellbeing. For many people, these impacts extend beyond the moment of movement. However, they remain difficult to quantify and are therefore often overlooked in decision-making. IDMC’s new methodology adapts established methods from public health and disaster economics to estimate these impacts, allowing them to be expressed as life-years lost and translated into monetary values or human costs. The methodology forms the basis of IDMC’s analysis of the human cost of disaster displacement in Bangladesh and can be adapted to other contexts to support evidence-based policy and investment decisions.

Country: World

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Mozambique: Humanitarian Snapshot, as of June 2026 [EN]
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Mozambique
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

Mozambique continued facing overlapping humanitarian challenges in June 2026, including conflict-related displacement in Cabo Delgado, the impacts of flooding, and growing concerns over drought and disease outbreaks.

Conflict-related displacement continued to drive humanitarian needs in Cabo Delgado Province. In June, approximately 12,174 people were displaced, bringing the total number of displaced people since the beginning of the year to 26,184. Movements remained largely localized within districts or to neighboring districts and were often recurrent in nature. Women and children accounted for 79% of the displaced people in June, while protection partners reported concerns related to family separation, gender-based violence, loss of documentation, and psychosocial distress. Although monthly displacement figures remained below 2025 levels for most of the year, the increase recorded in the last two months underscored the fragility of the security environment.

Humanitarian needs in conflict-affected areas continued to exceed available resources. The 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan requires US$348 million to address the most urgent needs of populations affected by conflict in northern Mozambique. By the middle of the year, donors had contributed US$116 million, representing 34 per cent of requirements. While funding has enabled the continuation of critical assistance, disparities remain across sectors. Funding disparities continued to affect the response, with only four sectors exceeding 30 per cent coverage: WASH (61%), Shelter/NFIs (48%), Health (50%) and Protection (37%). Coverage remained particularly low in Education (8%), Logistics (8%), Nutrition (11%), CCCM (19%) and FSL (21%). Funding shortfalls risk leaving vulnerable households without adequate support to meet their most urgent needs.

Communities affected by severe flooding earlier this year continued to face significant challenges. More than 417,000 people (67%) received some form of assistance, largely through food assistance, shelter and WASH interventions, against an estimated 620,000 people requiring support. While humanitarian assistance helped address immediate needs, recovery efforts remain critical in areas where livelihoods, agricultural production and essential infrastructure have yet to fully recover.

The Mozambique Institute of Meteorology has forecast below-average rainfall associated with El Niño between October 2026 and January 2027. In anticipation of the projected impacts on food security, agriculture and livelihoods, FAO and WFP launched their first joint anticipatory action appeal. Districts in Gaza Province, including Mapai, Massangena, Chigubo, Chicualacuala and Massingir, which were among the areas most affected by recent flooding, are now expected to face heightened drought-related risks, while Maputo City is being monitored for potential impacts.

Disease preparedness remained a priority as the cholera situation improved. Since September 2025, Mozambique has recorded more than 9,000 cases, with Nampula, Tete and Cabo Delgado accounting for most infections. The number of new cases continued to decline during June, reflecting a significant reduction in transmission compared with the peak of the outbreak. To strengthen preparedness for future outbreaks, the Emergency Relief Coordinator approved a Cholera Anticipatory Action Framework for Mozambique, which will enable early action in areas at risk before transmission escalates. Mozambique remains at a low alert level regarding the Ebola outbreak in the region and continues to monitor developments closely in coordination with health authorities and partners.

By mid-year, the Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan (HNRP) is 29 per cent funded out of the total requirements related to conflict and floods. Pooled funding mechanisms accounted for the majority of contributions, providing US$102.8 million, including the Mozambique Humanitarian Fund (US$83.3 million) and the Central Emergency Response Fund (US$14.5 million).
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Mozambique
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.
Mozambique continued facing overlapping humanitarian challenges in June 2026, including conflict-related displacement in Cabo Delgado, the impacts of flooding, and growing concerns over drought and disease outbreaks.
Conflict-related displacement continued to drive humanitarian needs in Cabo Delgado Province. In June, approximately 12,174 people were displaced, bringing the total number of displaced people since the beginning of the year to 26,184. Movements remained largely localized within districts or to neighboring districts and were often recurrent in nature. Women and children accounted for 79% of the displaced people in June, while protection partners reported concerns related to family separation, gender-based violence, loss of documentation, and psychosocial distress. Although monthly displacement figures remained below 2025 levels for most of the year, the increase recorded in the last two months underscored the fragility of the security environment.
Humanitarian needs in conflict-affected areas continued to exceed available resources. The 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan requires US$348 million to address the most urgent needs of populations affected by conflict in northern Mozambique. By the middle of the year, donors had contributed US$116 million, representing 34 per cent of requirements. While funding has enabled the continuation of critical assistance, disparities remain across sectors. Funding disparities continued to affect the response, with only four sectors exceeding 30 per cent coverage: WASH (61%), Shelter/NFIs (48%), Health (50%) and Protection (37%). Coverage remained particularly low in Education (8%), Logistics (8%), Nutrition (11%), CCCM (19%) and FSL (21%). Funding shortfalls risk leaving vulnerable households without adequate support to meet their most urgent needs.
Communities affected by severe flooding earlier this year continued to face significant challenges. More than 417,000 people (67%) received some form of assistance, largely through food assistance, shelter and WASH interventions, against an estimated 620,000 people requiring support. While humanitarian assistance helped address immediate needs, recovery efforts remain critical in areas where livelihoods, agricultural production and essential infrastructure have yet to fully recover.
The Mozambique Institute of Meteorology has forecast below-average rainfall associated with El Niño between October 2026 and January 2027. In anticipation of the projected impacts on food security, agriculture and livelihoods, FAO and WFP launched their first joint anticipatory action appeal. Districts in Gaza Province, including Mapai, Massangena, Chigubo, Chicualacuala and Massingir, which were among the areas most affected by recent flooding, are now expected to face heightened drought-related risks, while Maputo City is being monitored for potential impacts.
Disease preparedness remained a priority as the cholera situation improved. Since September 2025, Mozambique has recorded more than 9,000 cases, with Nampula, Tete and Cabo Delgado accounting for most infections. The number of new cases continued to decline during June, reflecting a significant reduction in transmission compared with the peak of the outbreak. To strengthen preparedness for future outbreaks, the Emergency Relief Coordinator approved a Cholera Anticipatory Action Framework for Mozambique, which will enable early action in areas at risk before transmission escalates. Mozambique remains at a low alert level regarding the Ebola outbreak in the region and continues to monitor developments closely in coordination with health authorities and partners.
By mid-year, the Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan (HNRP) is 29 per cent funded out of the total requirements related to conflict and floods. Pooled funding mechanisms accounted for the majority of contributions, providing US$102.8 million, including the Mozambique Humanitarian Fund (US$83.3 million) and the Central Emergency Response Fund (US$14.5 million).
